suggestedGuidanceUpdates.suggestion: Require completed feature sessions to persist a retrievable handoff artifact and transcript skeleton before a feature can be marked completed.
suggestedGuidanceUpdates.evidence: Hydrate-roundtrip scrutiny still had to reconstruct some completed feature evidence from git history because mission artifacts were missing or inconsistent for multiple worker sessions.

suggestedGuidanceUpdates.suggestion: Document that when GitNexus tooling is unavailable in the environment, workers should explicitly record the git-diff fallback in feature handoffs so later scrutiny does not need to infer why required GitNexus checks were absent.
suggestedGuidanceUpdates.evidence: Repo instructions require GitNexus impact/detect checks, but this Windows validation environment exposed no GitNexus tooling and workers repeatedly fell back to git diff/status review without consistent artifact notes.

rejectedObservations.observation: Add a library note describing repository::normalize_foreign_shapes as the canonical recursive foreign normalization seam.
rejectedObservations.reason: rejected because it is useful architectural knowledge but not required to execute current worker commands, and scrutiny should avoid extra documentation churn during validation synthesis.

rejectedObservations.observation: Retain a failing review entry for milestone-b-public-id-hydration-normalization in the hydrate-roundtrip synthesis.
rejectedObservations.reason: rejected because features.json now marks that feature as superseded/cancelled and the narrowing corrective feature owns the validated contract going forward.

rejectedObservations.observation: Retain a failing review entry for milestone-b-recursive-foreign-link-normalization in the hydrate-roundtrip synthesis.
rejectedObservations.reason: rejected because the current milestone queue replaces the original implementation claim with milestone-b-recursive-foreign-link-normalization-evidence-revalidation, whose completed evidence fixes the scrutiny gap for VAL-HYDRATE-003.
